title: Arkhipov on B-59 — One vote from nuclear war
location: Soviet Foxtrot-class submarine B-59, Sargasso Sea / Caribbean approaches
domain: Cold War nuclear crisis / command under extreme stress
known_outcome: Vasili Arkhipov refuses to authorize use of the nuclear torpedo. B-59 surfaces. The submarine is escorted out of the quarantine zone. Nuclear war does not begin that afternoon.
decision_question: With batteries dying, CO2 rising, and depth charges shaking the hull, does the flotilla staff officer join a launch authorization — or refuse?

opening.cold_open: It is the hottest day of the Cuban Missile Crisis under the sea. Temperature in compartments climbs past 110°F. Practice depth charges hammer the hull. B-59 has not heard from Moscow in days. Some officers believe war may already have started.
opening.what_they_knew: They knew U.S. ships were above them. They knew a nuclear-armed torpedo was among their weapons. They did not know the depth charges were intended as signaling charges, not a full attack. They did not have a clear picture of whether global war had begun.
opening.pressure: Heat, CO2, sleep debt, and the belief that silence from Moscow means catastrophe. Launch authority culture on a Foxtrot was not a Hollywood red-button myth — it was a human argument in a steel tube.

choices.label: Refuse — force surface and live
choices.summary: Arkhipov holds the line against launch. The boat surfaces into the American screen and is forced out of the area.
choices.immediate: No nuclear torpedo is fired. B-59 comes up. U.S. destroyers dominate the scene with conventional force.
choices.near_term: The crisis continues on the surface track of diplomacy. Kennedy and Khrushchev still have a path short of city-killing exchange.
choices.longer_arc: Later testimony and scholarship make Arkhipov's role a canonical near-miss of the nuclear age. The decision becomes a teaching case in restraint under incomplete information.
choices.image_prompt: interior of a cramped 1960s Soviet diesel submarine, exhausted officers, dim red lighting, depth charge shock, tense documentary still, no text
choices.vo_script: On October 27, 1962, three men on a Soviet submarine held the difference between a bad day and the end of cities. The historical path is restraint. Arkhipov does not authorize the nuclear shot. The boat surfaces. History continues.

choices.label: Delay the vote — demand another attempt to contact Moscow
choices.summary: SPECULATION: The council stalls for a radio attempt or a further battery assessment before any launch vote finalizes.
choices.immediate: Minutes stretch. Depth charges continue. Risk of accident or misread intent remains high even without a nuclear shot.
choices.near_term: A successful contact attempt could clarify that war has not started — or silence could harden the worst assumptions.
choices.longer_arc: This branch is dramatized process, not a recorded minute-by-minute alternate. It explores how thin the margin was between procedure and catastrophe.
choices.image_prompt: submarine radio room, operator straining through static, officers waiting, cold war tension, painterly documentary, no text
choices.vo_script: What if they waited for Moscow one more time? This is dramatized process, not a second history book. The air is still poison-hot. The charges still fall. Delay can save a world — or waste the last minutes before someone decides the war already began.

choices.label: Authorize the nuclear torpedo
choices.summary: SPECULATION: Launch authority aligns for a nuclear torpedo shot against the American screen. This is the nightmare branch.
choices.immediate: A nuclear detonation at sea against U.S. naval forces would almost certainly be read in Washington as the opening of general war.
choices.near_term: Escalation ladders that were still diplomatic could collapse into strategic exchange within hours.
choices.longer_arc: Any detailed 'who wins' story after this point is fantasy. The responsible claim is simple: this branch is the reason the historical refusal matters. Label: simulated catastrophe path.
choices.image_prompt: abstract restrained image of a submarine silhouette under destroyers, ominous sky, no mushroom cloud gore, serious historical warning still, no text
choices.vo_script: If the vote goes the other way, we leave the realm of what is known. A nuclear shot at sea on the worst day of the Missile Crisis is not a clever wargame ending — it is a plausible road to general war. We mark this branch as simulation, not history, because the only honest detail is the scale of the risk.

sources: Public historical accounts of B-59 / Vasili Arkhipov during the Cuban Missile Crisis
U.S. and Soviet secondary literature on the quarantine and submarine incidents of October 1962
ELOSTIRION corpus notes: decision-point framing; incomplete information; no hindsight smuggling

provenance.discipline: ELOSTIRION accuracy: decision-point framing; primary-source grounding; speculation labeled
provenance.notes: Baseline is refusal and surfacing. Launch and extended-delay branches are counterfactual and must remain labeled.

style_lock: cold war documentary realism, cramped metal interiors, heat and sweat implied, restrained palette, no text, no propaganda posters
